title: Conjecture 2
context_text: This conjecture naturally arises from Theorem 1, which shows every digraph has a majority 4-colouring. It would be best possible: odd directed cycles require 3 colours (since each vertex has outdegree 1, a majority colouring is a proper colouring), and circulant digraphs with large odd outdegree also witness this lower bound.

title: Conjecture 9
statement_text: For $k \geq 2$, every digraph has a vertex $(k+1)$-colouring such that for each vertex $v$, at most $\frac{1}{k}\deg^+(v)$ out-neighbours of $v$ receive the same colour as $v$.
context_text: This is the natural generalisation of Conjecture 2 to arbitrary $k$. The proof of Theorem 1 generalises to give an upper bound of $k^2$ colours; whether $O(k)$ colours suffice is explicitly stated as open. The conjecture is best possible, as shown by the $k$-th power of a directed $n$-cycle with $n \not\equiv 0 \pmod{k+1}$.
notes: PDF source — '$k > 2$' in extracted text likely renders '$k \geq 2$'; LaTeX corrected to $k \geq 2$ for mathematical consistency with the stated generalisation of Conjecture 2.

title: Open Problem 1
statement_text: Is there a constant $\beta < 1$ for which every digraph has a 3-colouring such that for every vertex $v$, at most $\beta \deg^+(v)$ out-neighbours receive the same colour as $v$?
context_text: Theorems 3 and 4 establish majority 3-colourings (i.e., $\beta = \frac{1}{2}$) under extra degree assumptions (logarithmic minimum outdegree, or bounded minimum/maximum degree ratio). Whether any $\beta < 1$ holds universally for 3-colourings without degree conditions is open.

title: Open Problem 2
statement_text: Does every tournament have a majority 3-colouring?
context_text: Ilya Bogdanov proved (in response to the original MathOverflow question [7]) that tournaments admit a majority colouring with a bounded number of colours, but whether 3 colours suffice for all tournaments is open.

title: Open Problem 3
statement_text: Does every Eulerian digraph have a majority 3-colouring?
context_text: For Eulerian digraphs $G$ where every vertex $v$ has equal in- and out-degree $\deg(v)$, Lovász's result for undirected graphs gives a majority 4-colouring, and an analogous argument yields a 3-colouring in which each vertex $v$ has at most $\frac{2}{3}\deg(v)$ same-colour in-or-out-neighbours, proving a special case of Open Problem 1. Whether a full majority 3-colouring (with $\frac{1}{2}\deg^+(v)$) exists for all Eulerian digraphs is open.

title: Open Problem 4
statement_text: Does every digraph in which every vertex has in-degree and out-degree $k$ have a majority 3-colouring?
context_text: A variant of Theorem 4 (using the Lovász Local Lemma) proves this for $k \geq 144$; the case of small $k$ is open.

title: Open Problem 5
statement_text: Is there a characterisation of digraphs that have a majority 2-colouring, or a polynomial-time algorithm to recognise such digraphs?
context_text: Majority 2-colourability is not closed under taking induced subdigraphs (an example with four vertices is given in the introduction), making a simple hereditary characterisation impossible; no structural characterisation or efficient recognition algorithm is known.

title: Open Problem 6
statement_text: Does every digraph have an $O(k)$-colouring such that for each vertex $v$, at most $\frac{1}{k}\deg^+(v)$ out-neighbours receive the same colour as $v$ (for all $k \geq 2$)?
context_text: The current upper bound from the proof of Theorem 1 is $k^2$ colours for the generalised Conjecture 9; it is explicitly noted as open whether $O(k)$ colours suffice.
notes: PDF source — '$k > 2$' in extracted text likely renders '$k \geq 2$'.

title: Open Problem 7
statement_text: A digraph $G$ is majority $c$-choosable if for every function $L : V(G) \to \mathcal{Z}$ with $|L(v)| \geq c$ for each vertex $v \in V(G)$, there is a majority colouring of $G$ with each vertex $v$ coloured from $L(v)$. Is every digraph majority $c$-choosable for some constant $c$?
context_text: Acyclic digraphs are shown to be majority 2-choosable (from the proof of Theorem 1), and Theorems 3 and 4 extend to the choosability setting; whether a universal constant $c$ covers all digraphs is open.
notes: PDF source — $\mathcal{Z}$ likely denotes the integers $\mathbb{Z}$ (list assignment).

title: Open Problem 8
statement_text: Let $S(G)$ be the set of all stable sets of a digraph $G$ and $S(G,v)$ the stable sets containing $v$. A fractional majority colouring assigns each $T \in S(G)$ a weight $x_T \geq 0$ such that $\sum_{T \in S(G,v)} x_T \geq 1$ for each vertex $v$. What is the minimum $k$ such that every digraph $G$ has a fractional majority colouring with $\sum_{T \in S(G)} x_T \leq k$? Perhaps $k < 3$.
context_text: This fractional relaxation of majority colouring is introduced in Section 5 as a natural variant. The informal conjecture that the minimum total weight is less than 3 would constitute a fractional analogue of Conjecture 2.
notes: The 'perhaps $k < 3$' is an informal conjectural remark embedded within the open problem statement.
